item.title: Socio-environmental Path of Indigenous Territories in the Venezuelan Amazon
Socio-environmental Analysis of the Venezuelan Amazon
item.description:
Organization: Asociación Civil Kape-Kape (October 2017 – February 2018)

Role: Researcher

Coordination of field activities, data collection, GIS construction, and technical report writing for the socio-environmental analysis of the Venezuelan Amazon region, working alongside indigenous base organizations and NGOs operating in the territory.

item.title: Cartographic Survey for Shiriana People's Traditional Territory Self-demarcation
item.description:
Organization: Escuela de Antropología, Universidad Central de Venezuela (January 2015 – December 2016)

Role: Technical Team Member

Design and application of cartographic survey methodologies to support the self-demarcation process of the Shiriana people’s traditional and ancestral territory. The project was framed within an anthropological advisory initiative for endogenous projects in indigenous communities, combining geographic and anthropological approaches.

item.title: Social Map of Indigenous Women Trafficking in Illegal Gold Mines of the Venezuelan Amazon
item.description:
Organization: Organización de mujeres indígenas amazónicas Wanaaleru (2015 – 2016)

Role: Technical Advisor

Design and application of methodologies for mapping illegal gold mining operations in the Venezuelan Amazon, with a focus on documenting indigenous women trafficking for sexual exploitation. The project produced a social map to support advocacy and territorial rights of indigenous communities.

item.title: Mapa Territorio Tearime Sirikoi Aerime Suititi. Deiyu Wachurijua (Pueblo en Aislamiento Voluntario) Sector Sipapo
Interculturality and Collective Health in Indigenous Communities of the Venezuelan Amazon
item.description:
Organization: Grupo de Estudios Antropológicos, Ciudad Bolívar, Venezuela (January 2013 – December 2013)

Role: Guest Researcher

Development of methodologies for georeferenced epidemiological data collection among the Anú, Barí, Cuiva, Hiwi, and Wayúu peoples. The project produced cartographic representations of epidemiological data and assessed the coverage and reach of the public health system in indigenous territories.
